sentences.text: It's 30 minutes already?
As I understand it, all of my family, I don't know where that came from, but I've heard so many times it might have been right, it seemed that everybody was taken to Buchenwald.
Yet, I learned later on that Buchenwald was for male Jews-- not-- not female Jews.
So I do believe that my brothers were in Buchenwald, and my sister-- sisters-- wound up in Auschwitz.
It's not confirmed.
I'm still searching today to find out if that's what it was, through the Red Cross.
[NON-ENGLISH], or [?
Claudine ?] now.
But yes, my older sister.
Well, we had no home to go to.
And we all remember that the underground was very active at that point, trying to save Jews.
So we contacted the underground person that had arranged for our escape to Switzerland.
I just-- just remembered something that I needed to tell you, is that at the border, when I told you that they had the names, they knew when, and they knew where.
We were denounced.
And I later came to find out that it was a member of our family.

But again, for the same reason that I-- I forgive my father for doing what he did to save his life, there were situations in which the Germans had promised any Jews that denounced other Jews would have another year, week, a month, a year, to live.
So this member of our family, because there was a trial later after the war, said as a reason and excuse that she was trying to save her own life.
I've heard tante, which is of course aunt, and that's impossible, because I know that it wasn't any one of my mother or father's brothers or sisters.
So I believe it was a first cousin.
The name escapes me now.
There was a trial.
There was-- that was quite an ordeal, which I might tell your story later.
That came after the war.
But that is why the Germans had our names.
But we contacted that person.
And in those days the underground was quite active in placing Jews in orphanages, in convents, in gentile Belgian families that their purpose was to save Jews privately and so on.
And we were then given the name of a Belgian gentile family that would have-- that was willing to hide us.
All three of us at the time, yes.
So we went there.

And a deal was struck because I'm afraid that not all Belgians were doing it to save Jews, or they cared about Jews, in fact, It's safe to say that the majority was just anti-Semitic and as bad to the Jews as-- as I found out later, that the Germans were.
So these people were quite willing to take us on for a fee.
And luckily enough, in those days, for whatever little that my father had put away, we had a little bit of money.
I understand also that the underground did subsidize somewhat the people with food and money for food, whoever was willing to hide Jews.
So I assume today that there was a combination of subsidizing from the underground, and the monies that we were able to give them.
However, that was a nightmare.
What were the names of this Belgian family?
Jeanne and Andre-- I'm sorry.
Jeanne and Emile Barniche.
They also had a son-- two sons actually-- one that was in the house while we were hiding.
His name was Ferdinand, who was my age by the way.
And then an older son.
And I forgot his name And you stayed at their home from-- what date did you arrive?
Well, it was from 1942, again, right onto the liberation in 1944.
We stayed at their home as a stretch as to what we had to endure.
63 Rueguillaume Stocq, in a community called Ixelles, which was in the city of Brussels, Belgium.
R-U-E-G-U-I-L-L-A-U-M-E, second word S-T-O-C-Q.
Rueguillaume Stocq.
Ixelles is spelled I-X-E-L-L-E-S.
Well, at first it was not.
We were hidden.
And for quite a few months it was necessary to change names or identity.
We were hidden in-- the best way that I can describe it, is a cellar, where the only light of day was there was a window about six inches protruding from the cellar to the sidewalk.
So that the only thing you could see from this particular window about six inches high was shoes, because that was the street level.
I would say that the room was maybe 12 by 12.
It was the three of us.
There were, I remember, two mattresses on the floor, a chair, and I believe there was a desk.
I seem to remember at least some kind-- kind of a table.
And it was cold tile floor.
I'll never forget that.
Very, very cold.
Very, very dark.
Very, very dingy.
And that is where we stayed all three of us for a few months.
At that time the only thing that was asked of us is, of course, to be quiet, not to speak, not to make any noise, not to ever come up to the Barniches' living quarters.
That meals would be brought to us.
And that was our lot.
When they remembered.
And what they considered meals were leftovers, if they were any.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was being in jail.
And these people never showed any care or emotion, or even let us know what was happening, or what was going on.
We knew nothing.
We-- all I could remember is my mother sitting there, and crying and praying for the loss of her two sons and daughters, with some hope someday that they would come back.
My sister and I, at least we were able to speak to each other.
But there came in time where these people decided that, well, that wasn't good enough for them.
And of course they collected their money, which is another story on how we got it to them.
But they decided that as long as we have able-bodied people there, and we were really trash anyway, that anything and everything that they wanted us to do, we would do.
Which meant cleaning, scrubbing, cooking, washing, all of the things that a servant would really do-- much more like a slave than a servant, very frankly.
That's what they would use us for.
Only my sister and I.
My mother was, at the time, her emotional state and her physical state wasn't very good.
The best that she could do was to just sit there and pray and cry.
That's all I remember my mother ever doing.
But my sister and I we're literally used as slaves in that house.
No.
For that we had to go on the second floor.
And that's really just about the only time we were able to go beyond the ground floor.
And that is, well, for certain hygienic reasons.
Other than that we had a pail is what we had for our bathroom facilities.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, there came a time where we knew that, or at least my sister at that time, was rebelling and was answering them back to a point that one day they actually shoved her out of the house and said well, be found out and be killed.
You're a Jew, and if you do this again this is exactly where you and your family will be.
She was thrown out.

And then they, I think with some reasoning, and the realization that whether they were getting money or their purposes were, that hiding Jews, whether for money or not for money, was a penalty of death, they decided to let her back in.
But we knew then, because of the fact that my sister was maybe not as young as I was, so I was able to take a lot more.

And the fact that they were using and abusing her a lot more than they were doing to me, that it-- it would have been impossible for her to stay there because this would have happened again and, of course, if she's discovered we would be discovered.
Well, my sister was-- was six years older than I was.
So at the time I would say she was 13, 14, and certainly able to understand a lot more of what was happening to us than I was.
[SIGHS] Although I was growing up very fast.
[CHUCKLES] At this point I'm dark on how we were able to contact the underground.
But I would assume it [? was the ?] Barniche, because they saw that she was becoming impossible to deal with, and very rebellious.
So the underground was contacted, and my sister was taken out of there and taken to a convent.
And the rest of my sister's story for the rest of the war is that all I know is she was taken to a convent and we were only reunited at liberation.
So my sister was safe, and no longer with us, and I was just left with my mother.
And there came a time where it was just as a-- as a child of eight years old, I guess, at that time, it was-- I began to understand everything.
And I began to realize who I was, what was happening, what was going on.
And so I asked, one day, since their son, who was my age, was-- I was playing with him once in awhile.
He did come down to the cellar.
He didn't know any better.
He didn't feel the way [? their ?] parents did.
Although later on, which is a different story, he used me to his advantage, too.
He said, why don't you come to school?
And I said, because I'm supposed to hide.
That's the best that I could do.
I can't just go to school, I'm not a Belgian.
I'm not-- he was going to Catholic school-- I'm not a Catholic.
And I know what happens to Jewish boys if they're discovered.
So I had that discussion a day or two later, or had mentioned it to the Barniche.
And I don't know.
It was one of their weak moments or whatever.
But they said, would you like to go to school.
I said, of course.
I mean, this to me was a release from jail, a release from a cell to just to get out and be able to see the sun.
I said, yes.
Yes, yes.
Well, we'll contact the-- the same people on the underground, who I'm sure they must have had contact on a weekly or a monthly basis, whenever it was necessary for them to get paid.

And one of the underground persons came down and talked to my mother and I, and said-- and explained that they probably could handle that-- if it was OK with the Barniche for me to adopt their names, and to be enrolled in a Catholic school, and hide my Jewish identity in every way.
And that would mean, of course, I was then a very dark-haired boy-- a dark-haired boy.
And I believe I must have looked somewhat Jewish.
I also had some mannerism, because, after all, in my family we spoke Yiddish, and I suppose the accent came through.
And certain mannerism and certain things that I needed to lose and forget.

So I do remember for one month straight on a daily basis, someone from the underground coming down to the cellar and teaching me the proper way, or the other way of not speaking French with this Jewish mannerism and accent.
They lightened my hair.
I remember that.
And it was decided then to enroll me in this Catholic school.
And this is how I finally got out of the cellar-- where I believe I was there for six, seven months-- and go daily to a Catholic school.
And I mean really Catholic, because daily I had to go to mass before going to class.
Yes.
The-- I had a birth certificates-- a birth certificate naming me as Andre Barniche, the son of Jeanne and Emile Barniche.
I do believe, however, that the school administration, for so many things that happened while I was in school, must have known.
They were priests.
And I think they were-- were willing to help wherever they could.
I don't know to this day if-- if that's absolutely true, except for one teacher, because he saved my life many times.
But I believe that the administration accepted these falsified papers with the full knowledge of who I was.
Because I also do remember, there were many searches by the SS and the Gestapo in the schools.
When they knew that Jewish children were enrolled in Catholic schools and hiding under guise with different names and so on.
And of course the searchers were-- and the only way they could have known, is to drop your pants and only Jewish boys was circumcised in those days.
And that's how they-- many, many children, I know one at least, a good friend of mine, was taken away because that happened.
But the administration seemed to know when those things were going to happen, or at least the principal did.

And I remember many times the principal walking into class and calling me out for whatever reason, and to wind up in the infirmary, or hidden in his office, or whatever, so that whenever there was a search they wouldn't see that I was a Jewish boy.
Well, again, it-- it would have been the end of 1942 into 1943 at that point.
I would say more 1943, because there were a few months of 1942 that I never got out, so yes, 1943.
Right through liberation.
The events in that Catholic school, I can't begin to tell you.
Well, actually what did happen to me, finally, because my Jewish upbringing at that age couldn't have been very strong.
My character was being formed and I was no longer being raised.
My father was dead.
My mother certainly was not in-- although she reminded me on a daily basis when I did go to Catholic school, never to forget that I was Jewish.
But to-- to forget it at the hours that I was in school.
So I was kind of-- kind of a mixture of trying to remember I was a Jew, and at the same time I was to forget it.
Otherwise it meant my life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I lost my train of thought.
I hope you'll forgive me.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, there was a teacher in the class.
And in those days, for all-- well, I think still today I guess, in elementary school, which is what it was, the-- the teacher was the same teacher all day long.
There wasn't a change of teachers.
They taught all the subjects.
I guess I must have been told by the principal, or must have known, or must have suspected something.
But I felt very close to this teacher.
And he always showed an interest in me, and a liking to me and so on.
And I remember one time where there was a search that had not been announced.
And we heard the German troops going from classes to classes to classes.
And we were one of the last ones that they were going to walk into.
And I said, oh my god.
This is where they're going to discover me and I have to trust someone.
And I went over to that teacher and I told him straight out-- I had no choice at that point-- I told them that I was Jewish and he seemed to already know it.
And could he do something for me?
And he wrote a pass and took me to the infirmary where I stayed through the search.
And that was one of those times that I was saved.
Do you remember this teacher's name?
[SHAKING HEAD] No.
Well, I was never really their son except on paper for the-- for the Catholic school.
What we had become is slaves.
They called us dirty Jews more than once.
And we were-- it was just miserable.
I mean, we were never, never allowed to either eat with them, be with them, stay with them.
Things that they were doing.
And I remember specifically, because that made such an impression on me.
They had a son, as I said, my age.
And that son, at-- well Christmas was not celebrated really in Belgium.
It was more Saint Nicholas.
And those were the times where children were getting gifts and so on.
And Emile Barniche, the man at the house, was, besides being a conductor-- a tram conductor-- also had a hobby.
And I guess it augmented his-- his income, by making little toys for Saint Nicholas Day.
And I always remember him making toys for his son, but not for me.
And I wasn't allowed to have them.
And I wasn't allowed to touch them.
And that must have made quite an impression on me, because I asked for one.
And of course I was refused.

And to jump over after the war, many, many years later, and only a few years ago, I used to have some awful nightmares, and wake up in the middle of the night with two things always, always coming about.
Images of-- one was a little horse-drawn carriage.
And another thing was the-- the picture of, in French is called a fleur-de-lis.
It's-- it's a design; looks like a flower.
And every time I woke up screaming, my wife tells me, it was always with that image in mind.
I was always wondering what it was.

And now, I came to find out, because I did visit the Barniche after the war-- many years after the war--I think about 30 years after the war; it took me that long to have the guts to do that-- was that the little horse-drawn carriage that I always wanted that he had given to his son [? who has ?] denied me.

And that some reason it made such an impression on me that it continued right onto my adulthood and after the war as being one of the images that was very, very strong, and woke up in-- in cold sweat and screams.
But our lives there, all I can remember is that this is, in my young years, this is the first time I wished for being taken or dying, and no longer having this nightmare.
And seeing my mother that way, it was just the most awful thing there was.
But there was so many things in between that that happened.
[SIGHS] As I said, the monies that were either paid by the underground wasn't enough.
And we were paying them monthly.
And that was dwindling.
It was a gold coin, I remember, on a monthly basis.
That's the one thing I couldn't even begin to tell you.
And I don't even know what the gold coins, from what country they were.
I just remembered these gold coins.
Which, to go back somewhat-- some-- I buried in the garden of the house-- we-- the house that was denied back to us by those gentiles.
It was buried in the backyard, because I knew that place best.
And whenever it was necessary to pay them, in view of the fact that if we had had the whole money with us, they would have taken it away from us, and then we would be of no further use to them.
It was on a monthly basis that I used to go to the house and unbury one piece and go ahead and pay them.
But I saw that dwindling.
And there came a time, close to the time of liberation, 1944, [INAUDIBLE], that we were going to run out of this money.
And we didn't know what was going to be, what was going to happen to us once this money ran out.
We knew very well that they weren't doing it because they wanted to save us.
We knew their feeling then.
We knew what they had put us through.
We knew what we were going through.
And by the way, in the middle of all this. to go back to the school, I had become quite the Catholic.
I, since I was going to mass daily, and it was Catholic priests that were teaching the classes and so on.
It was no longer something I was doing by design.

I believed in it, and I believed in it very strongly, to a point I remember that one of the games, if you want to call it games, that the kids played on the street, whenever there was a allied bombing-- Whether it was the British or the Americans that were bombing Brussels, Belgium-- we were hiding in doorways-- not then shelters.
And the biggest thing was to pick up the shrapnel-- a collection of shrapnel for us.
And whatever guns were left on the streets by the Germans as they were running for cover.
And so we had-- I had amassed quite an arsenal of guns-- which I thought at one time may be necessary-- and shrapnels and so on.
And Barniches' son, who was my age, who was also a Catholic boy, and a choir boy, asked me that, you know, if something ever happens to you in a bombing, you're-- you're certainly not Jewish.
You're Catholic.
But you've never been baptized.
I said, well, I think God would understand, you know.
And he says, oh, no.
You have to be baptized to go to Heaven.
And I do remember that during an Allied bombing he, as a choir boy, took the right to baptize me in a doorway during an Allied bombing.
That made quite an impression on me.
And now I felt that if anything happens to me, I will go to Heaven.
I'm not Catholic and I'm one of them.
[SIGHS] What was one of the occurrences.
Fernand.
Fernand Barniche.
Fernand.
F-E-R-N-A-N-D.
Fernand Barniche.
[SIGHS] Yeah.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes.
And believed you would go to Heaven.
Time came where, as I said, the money was running out.
Very much so.
What happened is, first of all, it happened to be the house where the Belgians had taken over, and where my brother was arrested.
So I only did it in the night.
And what was very-- the only way that I could have done that.
And the house was situated in such a way that, as I said, we had a factory downstairs in the cellar.
And we had a store upstairs.
But it-- it was not a storefront as we know it here.
It was a vestibule.
You had to go into a vestibule.
The store would have been a right door to go into the store.
But if you continued down that vestibule you would go into the backyard.
And it is in that backyard that I had buried the money.
And so to go there, in a backyard, it wasn't necessary to enter the store, which had a closed door, because they wanted to close the door from the residents of the house.
So at night, and after hours, where the front door on the street was always open, because it was a store, I was able to enter that, and make sure that the store doors were closed.
And so I wouldn't be uncovered.
And go in the backyard, take out one, and come on out again.
There was a time, however, where Fernand had followed me, because he had seen that I had done that.
And he watched me.
And he came out and confronted me with that.
And although he was-- played with me.
He wasn't necessarily my friend.
He called me dirty Jew many times.
He, as a kid that age, would do at the time, he says, you know, if you don't do what I tell you to do, I will tell on you.
And that scared me to no end.
And of course what he had me do for the rest of the time that I was with the Barniche is his homework.
Any studies that he had to do, anything that his father and mother had told him to do, became my duties.
